data.title: Tissue Titans Unite: Suzano, Kimberly-Clark Form $3.4 Billion Global JV
data.description:
Suzano S.A. SUZ rolls up $3.4 billion in a tissue deal with Kimberly-Clark Corporation KMB.

São Paulo, Brazil-based Suzano will pay Kimberly-Clark $1.734 billion in cash for its stake in the joint venture.

This newly formed company will produce and distribute products such as toilet paper and facial tissues across 70 countries.

Suzano will hold a 51% stake in the venture, while Kimberly-Clark will retain a 49% share.

The joint venture is incorporated in the Netherlands. It will employ around 9,000 people and encompass 22 manufacturing facilities across 14 countries in regions.

These sites collectively have a production capacity of roughly 1 million tonnes of tissue products annually. They have generated around $3.3 billion in net sales in 2024.

Also Read: Kimberly-Clark Commits $2 Billion For US Expansion Overhaul

Additionally, the agreement grants Suzano a call option, allowing it to purchase Kimberly-Clark’s remaining 49% stake in the joint venture.

Post closure, Kimberly-Clark expects an estimated dilution of approximately 30 to 40 cents to adjusted EPS in the first full year after closing.

The transaction is expected to be finalized by mid-2026, pending regulatory and governmental approvals, customary closing conditions, and the completion of Kimberly-Clark’s corporate restructuring.

Upon completion of the transaction, Suzano and Kimberly-Clark will formalize a joint venture agreement outlining their respective rights and responsibilities regarding the governance, ownership, operations, and management of the new company.

Kimberly-Clark CEO Mike Hsu said, “Following years of deliberate investments that have strengthened Kimberly-Clark and IFP, we are excited to expand our partnership with Suzano and focus Kimberly-Clark’s portfolio on our higher growth, higher margin businesses.”

As of March 31, Kimberly-Clark’s cash and equivalents at quarter end was $563 million.

Price Action: SUZ shares are up 6.24% at $9.36 on Thursday’s last check.

  • Kimberly-Clark Analysts Slash Their Forecasts Following Weak Sales

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Bitcoin, Ethereum, XRP, Dogecoin Dip, But Trader Says Everything's Going According to Plan
data.description:
Major cryptocurrencies are dipping on Thursday morning as market participants wait for an announced phone call between President Donald Trump and China’s president Xi Jinping.

Notable Statistics:

  • IntoTheBlock data shows Bitcoin and Ethereum large transaction volume decreased 11.3% and 17.8%. Daily active addresses went down by 14.6% and 5.5%, respectively.
  • Coinglass data shows 93,731 traders were liquidated in the past 24 hours for $229.60 million.
  • SoSoValue data shows net inflows of $86.9 million into spot Bitcoin ETFs. Spot Ethereum ETFs saw net inflows of $56.98 million.

Trader Notes: Crypto trader Jelle notes that Bitcoin is consolidating between key EMAs and holding local support, exactly as expected. He anticipates more sideways action before a breakout to the upside.

Michael van de Poppe sees a potential liquidity sweep coming, predicting Bitcoin could dip below recent lows to flush weak hands before reversing sharply and setting the stage for a stronger upward move.

Disclosure: 82% of retail CFD accounts lose money

On Solana, More Crypto Online says the altcoin remains in a corrective phase, struggling below the $164.50 resistance. The next support to watch is $143.50, and a confirmed 5-wave impulse is needed to flip the bias back to bullish.

Meanwhile, Kong Trading stated that Ethereum continues to look attractive amid consistent ETH ETF inflows.

For Dogecoin, Trader Tardigrade highlights a historical pattern where DOGE/BTC makes lower lows while DOGE trends upward, a signal that has preceded every major DOGE pump. That setup appears to be repeating now..

  • Bitcoin Stuck At $105,000: What Does Technical Analysis Say?

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Sustainable Style: How AI Is Reducing Waste In The Fashion World
data.description:
The fashion industry, renowned for its creative flair, is also a major contributor to environmental damage. The statistics are truly alarming. The industry accounts for 10% of global carbon emissions, surpassing the environmental toll of all international flights and maritime shipping combined. Additionally, it is the second-largest consumer of the world's water resources, utilizing a staggering 93 billion cubic meters annually, as reported by the World Bank. Compounding this issue, the fashion industry is responsible for producing 20% of global wastewater and 35% of all microplastics found in our oceans.

These figures underscore the significant environmental impact of the fashion sector, highlighting the ongoing need for the industry to adopt more environmentally sustainable practices. The vibrant creativity that defines fashion must be balanced with a concerted effort to mitigate its detrimental effects on the planet, ensuring a more eco-friendly future for the industry and the world.

What Is Sustainable Fashion?

Sustainable style refers to a fashion approach that prioritizes environmental and social responsibility throughout the entire lifecycle of clothing and accessories. It encompasses the use of eco-friendly materials, ethical production practices, and a commitment to reducing waste. Sustainable style aims to minimize the negative impact of fashion on the planet by promoting practices such as recycling, upcycling, and the use of renewable resources.

Far exceeding a simple fashion trend, this represents a worldwide commitment to change. The UN Alliance for Sustainable Fashion brings together various UN agencies and partner organizations to transform the fashion industry in alignment with global Sustainable Development Goals. The Alliance serves as a coordinator between different UN entities involved in fashion while advancing initiatives and regulations that ensure the entire fashion supply chain – from production to consumption – helps achieve specific sustainability targets set by the UN.

Source: UN Alliance for Sustainable Fashion Instagram

Additionally, fashion sustainability encourages consumers to make mindful purchasing decisions, favoring quality over quantity. By integrating sustainability into fashion, the industry can foster a more harmonious relationship with the environment while supporting fair labor practices and community development.

AI: The Key to Fashion's Environmental Progress

Enter artificial intelligence (AI) and new inroads paving the way for a more sustainable future in fashion. By optimizing production processes, reducing waste, and enhancing supply chain transparency, AI is revolutionizing how brands approach sustainability. From predictive analytics that forecast trends to smart inventory management systems that minimize overproduction, AI is not just a tool but a catalyst for change. 

Demand Forecasting Gets Smarter with Artificial Intelligence

AI demand forecasting integrates advanced technology with strategic planning to enhance logistical operations. Unlike traditional forecasting methods that solely rely on order data and past sales, AI-enabled forecasting takes a more comprehensive approach by analyzing diverse data sources, including current market trends, digital footprints, social media activity, and online reviews. 

These systems continuously process and learn from real-time data, enabling businesses to identify patterns and anticipate shifts in demand with greater precision. Through machine learning algorithms, AI systems can adapt and evolve with each new dataset, incorporating multiple variables like demographics, weather patterns, and social media trends to generate more nuanced and accurate forecasts. This holistic integration of AI technology not only streamlines the forecasting process but also provides businesses with predictive insights that were previously unattainable through conventional methods.

A study by Business of Fashion revealed that brands using AI for demand forecasting have seen an 85% improvement in forecast accuracy. This, in turn, has brought about an average  50% reduction in excess inventory, and a 30% decrease in storage costs. 

According to H&M Group's Sustainability Director Sarah Chen, AI technology has enabled their company to cut overproduction by 30% in just one year, as reported in their 2023 Sustainability Report. Her numbers are consistent with what the industry is seeing as a whole, where AI-powered demand forecasting has helped reduce fashion industry waste by 20-30%.

Intelligent Personalization Tools Transform Consumer Interaction

AI-powered personalization is dramatically transforming consumer engagement in sustainable fashion, according to recent Accenture research. The data reveals remarkable improvements across multiple metrics, with customer engagement showing a substantial 78% increase through AI-enabled interactions. The technology has proven particularly effective in promoting eco-friendly products, driving conversion rates up by 45% as consumers respond positively to personalized sustainable recommendations.

Customer satisfaction scores have also seen significant growth, with a 65% improvement reported after implementing AI-powered solutions. These impressive results demonstrate how AI technology is not only enhancing the shopping experience but also effectively connecting environmentally conscious consumers with sustainable products. The success can be attributed to AI's ability to deliver hyper-personalized experiences that cut through the noise and simplify decision-making for consumers, while simultaneously promoting sustainable fashion choices.

AI Transforms Supply Chain Efficiency

Fashion brands that leverage AI for supply chain optimization are fundamentally changing how they monitor and run their operations. AI-driven design tools can optimize patterns to reduce fabric waste during production. Additionally, AI-powered sorting systems in recycling facilities can accurately identify and separate materials, improving the quality of recycled textiles. By leveraging these technologies, fashion brands can significantly decrease their environmental footprint, promote circularity, and contribute to a more sustainable future, ultimately transforming the way we think about fashion consumption and waste.

The implementation of AI-driven supply chain optimization has yielded impressive results across multiple sustainability metrics. Dr. Emma Williams, Head of Sustainable Fashion Research at Stanford University, reports fashion supply chains optimized by AI showing a 45% reduction in overproduction and a 30% decrease in logistics-related carbon emissions. 

As Michael Roberts, Chief Technology Officer at Zara, emphasizes in the 2023 Inditex Annual Report, "AI has revolutionized how we monitor our supply chain sustainability. We can now track environmental impact in real-time across 1000+ suppliers." This real-time monitoring capability represents a significant leap forward in supply chain management and environmental stewardship.

Perhaps most notably, supplier compliance monitoring has improved, ensuring better adherence to environmental and ethical standards throughout the supply chain. These achievements demonstrate how AI technology is not just enhancing operational efficiency but also driving meaningful progress in sustainable fashion practices. 

Try-Before-You-Buy Tech Reduces Overconsumption

Virtual try-on technology is emerging as a game-changing solution in sustainable fashion retail, driving significant improvements in customer satisfaction and operational efficiency. 42% of shoppers say they’d like the ability to try on clothes virtually. By allowing customers to visualize clothing items on themselves through digital platforms before making a purchase, this AI is fueling a reduction in return rates. This substantial decrease not only improves the customer experience but also contributes to sustainability efforts by reducing the environmental impact associated with shipping, packaging, and processing returns.

Source: Future of Commerce

Chanel has revolutionized its digital shopping experience by implementing cutting-edge augmented reality (AR) and AI technology into its customer interface. The luxury fashion house now offers an immersive virtual testing platform where consumers can experiment with various products, from eyewear to cosmetics, through their personal devices. This sophisticated digital try-on solution not only enhances the online shopping journey but also empowers customers to make more confident purchasing decisions. By bridging the gap between digital and physical retail experiences, Chanel's AR technology has successfully minimized purchase uncertainty while elevating customer satisfaction levels in the luxury e-commerce space.

The successful implementation of virtual try-on solutions demonstrates how technological innovation can simultaneously address both business challenges and environmental concerns. By providing customers with more accurate and realistic previews of their potential purchases, retailers can minimize the likelihood of returns while enhancing the overall shopping experience. This technology represents a pivotal advancement in sustainable fashion retail, effectively bridging the gap between online shopping convenience and the traditional in-store fitting room experience.

AI Powers the Future of Sustainable Product Development

Artificial Intelligence is fundamentally transforming sustainable product design by enabling designers to optimize materials, predict environmental impact, and create more efficient production processes. According to a report by McKinsey, AI-powered design tools can reduce product development time by up to 50% while simultaneously improving sustainability metrics."AI is not just accelerating the design process – it's helping us make better, more sustainable choices from the very beginning," says Sarah Thompson, Head of Sustainable Design at Nike. "Our AI algorithms can analyze hundreds of material combinations and their environmental impact in minutes, a process that would take humans weeks or months." Nike's use of AI in design has contributed to a 30% reduction in their carbon footprint for new product lines.

Material innovation represents another crucial advancement. AI systems can now predict the performance of sustainable materials before they're physically produced. For instance, Adidas, in partnership with AI firm Alchemie Technology, developed a digital dyeing process that reduces water usage by 95% compared to traditional methods. "The combination of AI and sustainable design is helping us reach our goal of ending plastic waste," notes James Carnes, VP of Brand Strategy at Adidas.

European luxury brand Stella McCartney employs Google Cloud's AI to track their raw materials' environmental impact, resulting in a 20% reduction in their supply chain emissions. "AI gives us unprecedented visibility into our environmental impact, allowing us to make data-driven decisions that benefit both our business and the planet," explains Stella McCartney.

AI Fashion Tech: Navigating the Ethics Challenge

As the fashion industry increasingly integrates artificial intelligence (AI) to enhance sustainability, ethical concerns arise that warrant careful consideration. One major issue is the potential for job displacement, as automation may replace traditional roles within the industry. Additionally, the reliance on AI algorithms can perpetuate biases, leading to a lack of diversity in design and representation. Furthermore, the environmental impact of AI technologies themselves, such as energy consumption and e-waste, must be addressed. It is crucial for stakeholders to navigate these ethical dilemmas thoughtfully, ensuring that the pursuit of sustainability does not compromise social responsibility or inclusivity in fashion.

"The fashion industry must approach AI adoption with a holistic ethical framework," notes Professor Marcus Rodriguez, Head of Sustainable Fashion at Central Saint Martins. "We're seeing companies rush to implement AI solutions without fully considering their broader societal impact. The key is to harness AI's potential while preserving human expertise and ensuring inclusive, environmentally responsible practices."

These insights highlight the delicate balance between technological advancement and ethical responsibility in fashion's digital transformation. The industry must navigate these challenges thoughtfully, ensuring that the pursuit of sustainability through AI aligns with social responsibility and inclusive practices.

Looking Forward

The future of artificial intelligence in sustainable fashion is showing remarkable potential. McKinsey analysts believe that Generative AI could add up to $275 billion in profits to the fashion industry by 2030. Perhaps most impressively, the industry is expected to achieve a 35% reduction in overall waste by 2026, demonstrating AI's tangible impact on environmental sustainability. 

As Dr. Emma Thompson, a Fashion Technology Researcher at MIT, notes in the MIT Technology Review, "AI isn't just transforming fashion; it's revolutionizing how we approach sustainability in the entire retail ecosystem." This expert perspective underscores how AI is not merely enhancing existing processes but fundamentally reshaping the fashion industry's approach to sustainability and environmental responsibility.

Conclusion

The integration of AI into the fashion industry presents a transformative opportunity to address the pressing issue of waste. By leveraging advanced algorithms and data analytics, brands can optimize production processes, reduce overstock, and enhance supply chain efficiency. AI-driven insights enable designers to create more sustainable collections that align with consumer demand, minimizing environmental impact. As the industry embraces these innovative technologies, we can look forward to a future where fashion is not only stylish but also responsible. The journey towards sustainability is ongoing, and AI is poised to be a pivotal ally in reshaping the fashion landscape for the better.

Feature Image: DepositPhotos Licensed
